profile.behavior: Ground-foraging sparrow that scratches through leaf litter for seeds, berries, and invertebrates; often feeds in flocks and sings a clear, whistled song.
field_marks: Large, full-bodied sparrow with a rounded head, fairly prominent conical bill, long legs, and a long narrow tail.
Crisp white throat, sharply bordered by dark malar (whisker) stripes.
Bold head pattern: black-and-white crown and eyebrow stripes, or a softer tan-and-brown striped form.
Bright yellow lores between the eye and bill.
Brown, streaked back and wings with pale wing bars; underparts gray to pale gray.
profile.habitat: Forests and woodland edges, brushy regrowth, thickets, and pond or bog margins; also uses low shrubby cover and open ground nearby for foraging.

sources.title: Cornell Lab of Ornithology — White-throated Sparrow Identification
Smithsonian's National Zoo and Conservation Biology Institute — White-throated Sparrow

findings: Independent verification confirms the identity: White-throated Sparrow (Zonotrichia albicollis), family Passerellidae. The plate's length, weight, and wingspan match Cornell exactly.

All visible factual text is supported: ground scratching/foraging, flocking, whistled song, woodland-edge/thicket/bog-edge habitat, white throat with dark malar border, yellow lores, striped head morphs, gray bill, pinkish legs, brown streaked upperparts, gray underparts, and pale wing bars.

The primary bird has one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and an anatomically implied pair of wings; no extra or missing body parts are depicted. Its illustrated bill is somewhat long and pointed, and the feet/claws are slightly oversized and stylized versus the reference photos, but these are minor non-material deviations.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the plate.
